Peak INR in the immediate postop period captures worst coagulopathy but is often transient after bypass and transfusion. It is an indirect severity marker with limited renal specificity.

Peak INR closer to outcome may reflect ongoing shock/liver dysfunction or anticoagulation effects associated with worse organ perfusion and AKI risk. Extreme (max) is more informative than mean, but remains an indirect proxy.
Minimum INR is generally not pathologic and often reflects baseline/anticoagulation status rather than organ dysfunction. Minimal renal relevance and low prognostic proximity to creatinine rise.
Minimum INR in this window is unlikely to capture a harmful exposure and is weakly related to renal outcomes. Provides little mechanistic or prognostic information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

INR measurement frequency is largely a care-process indicator (bleeding concern, anticoagulation management) rather than renal physiology. Early postop monitoring is routine, so it has low specificity for predicting creatinine ratio.

Whether INR was checked reflects bleeding/anticoagulation concern and overall instability rather than renal physiology. As a monitoring-intensity proxy it has only weak, indirect association with later creatinine rise.

INR measurement frequency late may indicate ongoing bleeding risk, hepatic dysfunction, or anticoagulation management, not a direct AKI pathway. Still primarily a care-process proxy, so limited value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean eosinophil percentage early post-op is a nonspecific inflammatory/allergic marker and is not a typical driver of post-cardiac surgery AKI. The mean in the immediate post-op window is unlikely to add meaningful signal for creatinine at 60h.

Eosinophil percentage is generally nonspecific and only rarely relevant to AKI (e.g., drug-induced interstitial nephritis), which is uncommon and delayed in this setting. Mean aggregation further dilutes any transient signal.
A peak eosinophil percentage in the first 12h is usually noise from perioperative stress/leukocyte shifts rather than renal injury. Not a recognized near-term precursor of CSA-AKI.

A higher eosinophil peak could weakly proxy drug reaction/inflammation, but this is an uncommon mechanism for early post-cardiac surgery creatinine rise. Slightly more informative than 0–12h, yet still indirect.
Low eosinophil percentage early post-op mainly reflects stress leukogram/catecholamine effect and has no specific renal linkage. Minimum values are not clinically meaningful for AKI prediction here.
Minimum eosinophil percentage remains a nonspecific stress marker without a clear AKI mechanism. Limited prognostic proximity to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether eosinophils were measured is largely a proxy for CBC ordering/monitoring intensity rather than kidney injury. Per rules, _measured variables are low value unless tightly linked to renal concern.
Measurement presence reflects routine lab surveillance and clinician behavior more than renal pathophysiology. Weak and redundant as a predictor of creatinine change.
Late measurement still indicates lab monitoring rather than a renal mechanism, and eosinophils are not central to CSA-AKI prediction. Kept low due to strong proxy penalty for _measured features.
LDL measurement is unrelated to acute renal perfusion or evolving AKI and mainly reflects chronic risk assessment or incidental ordering. As a binary measured flag, it is a weak care-process proxy.
Checking LDL in the ICU is not mechanistically tied to postoperative creatinine rise and likely reflects non-renal workflow. Binary measurement indicator provides minimal predictive value.
LDL measurement late post-op remains clinically disconnected from AKI pathways and is mostly noise/ordering behavior. Per _measured rule, should score at the floor.

MCV reflects baseline anemia/macrocytosis (nutrition, liver disease, marrow status) and can weakly proxy frailty or chronic illness affecting renal reserve. Early post-op mean MCV is relatively stable and only indirectly related to AKI risk.

MCV remains a chronic hematologic marker with limited causal proximity to postoperative AKI, though it may correlate with comorbidity burden. The 13–24h window adds little new renal-specific information beyond baseline.

Mean corpuscular volume (MCV) reflects RBC size and anemia/nutrition/alcohol/B12/folate status rather than renal function. Even in the closest window, it is an indirect, nonspecific marker with little mechanistic linkage to AKI/creatinine rise.

Peak MCV in the immediate postop period mainly reflects baseline hematologic characteristics or transfusion-related shifts, not kidney injury. Early extremes are unlikely to be causally proximal to Hour-60 creatinine changes.
MCV maximum during 13–24h is still a nonspecific hematology signal and not a renal perfusion or injury marker. Any association would be weak and largely confounded by transfusion/illness context.
Late-window MCV maximum remains a poor renal predictor and does not capture AKI pathways. Extremes here do not meaningfully encode renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxic exposure.

Minimum MCV shortly after surgery is typically baseline variation or dilution/transfusion effect rather than kidney-specific physiology. It is temporally and mechanistically distant from creatinine trajectory.
MCV minimum in 13–24h is not kidney-specific and is a weak proxy at best for overall illness or transfusion practices. It offers little incremental information for predicting creatinine_ratio.
Even close to the outcome, low MCV reflects chronic microcytosis/iron deficiency more than evolving AKI. Renal specificity and prognostic proximity are minimal.

This is measurement frequency of MCV, which mainly reflects lab ordering/monitoring intensity rather than physiology. As a care-process proxy in the immediate postop window, it has minimal renal relevance.
MCV measurement count is a clinician-behavior proxy and not a kidney injury marker. Any signal would be nonspecific (general acuity) and weak for Hour-60 creatinine_ratio.

More frequent CBC/indices checks in 25–36h may weakly indicate ongoing instability/bleeding/transfusion that can correlate with AKI risk, but it is still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy. Per rules, measured variables should remain low.

Mean platelet volume (MPV) reflects platelet activation/inflammation and can correlate with systemic stress after cardiopulmonary bypass, indirectly relating to AKI risk. In 0–12h it is heavily influenced by transient postop effects, limiting specificity.

Average MPV in 13–24h may capture ongoing inflammatory/consumptive physiology that modestly associates with AKI, but it is not a direct renal biomarker. Mean aggregation may dilute short clinically relevant extremes.

Sustained elevation in MPV in the 25–36h window can reflect ongoing inflammation/platelet activation and microvascular dysfunction, which has a plausible link to postoperative AKI. Still indirect and nonspecific compared with hemodynamics/renal labs.
Peak MPV early after surgery may reflect acute inflammatory response to bypass and transfusion rather than kidney-specific injury. Temporal proximity is low and early extremes are often transient.

MPV maximum in 13–24h may better capture a pathologic inflammatory/consumptive peak than the mean and can modestly relate to AKI risk. However, it remains an indirect systemic marker with limited renal specificity.

A high MPV peak in 25–36h could indicate ongoing inflammatory/microthrombotic stress closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome, making it a moderate predictor. It is still not a direct renal perfusion or kidney injury biomarker, so not top-tier.

Minimum MPV in the immediate postop window is a nonspecific hematologic/inflammatory marker and not kidney-specific. Early extremes are common after CPB/transfusion and have weak, indirect linkage to Hour-60 creatinine.

Minimum MPV during 13–24h remains a nonspecific platelet index with limited mechanistic connection to AKI. Any association is indirect (inflammation/illness severity) and typically weak for predicting creatinine ratio.

Minimum MPV closer to the outcome could weakly reflect ongoing inflammation or consumptive processes that correlate with organ dysfunction. Still not renal-specific and likely redundant with stronger perfusion/biochemical predictors.

Measurement count/frequency is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy rather than physiology. It does not directly encode renal perfusion or injury and should be low value for creatinine ratio prediction.
MPV measured frequency mainly reflects clinician behavior/lab ordering patterns. It is not a renal mechanism and is unlikely to add independent signal beyond overall acuity.
Even near the outcome window, MPV measurement frequency is a care-process proxy and not kidney-specific. Per rules, _measured variables should remain low importance.

Mean lactate reflects global hypoperfusion/anaerobic metabolism after surgery, which is mechanistically linked to renal ischemia risk. In 0–12h it can be transient from CPB/resuscitation, so predictive value is moderate.
Persistently elevated mean lactate in 13–24h suggests ongoing shock or inadequate perfusion, a strong precursor to AKI. This trajectory window is more prognostic than immediate postop values.
Peak lactate captures worst hypoperfusion, which can precipitate renal ischemic injury. However, early peaks are common immediately post-CPB and may overstate sustained renal risk.

A high lactate maximum in 13–24h indicates significant ongoing or recurrent hypoperfusion, closely tied to subsequent creatinine rise. Extremes are clinically meaningful for AKI risk and outperform means when insults are episodic.

Minimum lactate mainly reflects best perfusion state and is less informative for capturing hypoperfusion insults that drive AKI. In the immediate postop period it is especially prone to being reassuring despite earlier peaks.
Minimum lactate in 13–24h is a weaker summary than peak or mean for renal risk because it ignores intermittent shock. It may add limited incremental information beyond other lactate aggregations.
Lactate measurement frequency is partly a proxy for instability/shock concern rather than a direct biomarker. It can weakly signal higher acuity early post-op but is not kidney-specific.
More frequent lactate checks in 13–24h often reflect ongoing hemodynamic concern and could correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy, so importance remains low.
Near 25–36h, frequent lactate monitoring may indicate persistent shock or deterioration, indirectly relevant to renal outcomes. Nonetheless it is a process/acuity proxy and should not score above 2.

Mean alkaline phosphatase is a hepatobiliary/bone marker with minimal direct relationship to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Early postoperative changes are nonspecific and unlikely to predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Alkaline phosphatase reflects hepatobiliary/bone turnover rather than renal function or perfusion. Early postoperative peaks are nonspecific and add little direct signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum alkaline phosphatase is not mechanistically linked to AKI pathways and is largely unrelated to renal reserve or perfusion. The 0–12h window is also prone to transient, non-renal fluctuations.
This mainly captures whether/ how often clinicians checked ALP (monitoring intensity) rather than a renal mechanism. Measurement frequency for ALP is not a typical AKI-driven behavior signal.

ALP measurement frequency in this window is a care-process proxy (liver workup/monitoring) with minimal renal specificity. It is unlikely to add independent predictive value for creatinine rise at 60h.

Even close to the outcome, ALP measurement frequency is still primarily a clinician-behavior proxy and not a direct kidney injury/perfusion marker. It should not be expected to track evolving AKI in a specific way.

RDW reflects chronic inflammation, anemia/iron handling, and overall physiologic reserve, which can correlate with postoperative complications including AKI but indirectly. Mean in 0–12h is largely baseline-like and not a near-term renal signal.

RDW is a nonspecific prognostic marker (frailty/inflammation) with only indirect linkage to renal injury. The 13–24h mean adds limited incremental information for Hour-60 creatinine beyond general illness severity.

Later RDW mean may reflect ongoing inflammation/bleeding/transfusion burden, which can associate with AKI risk but remains nonspecific. Mean aggregation also dilutes any short-lived perioperative perturbations.

A higher RDW max may capture baseline anisocytosis or early transfusion-related shifts, which are weakly associated with worse outcomes including AKI. In the immediate postoperative window it is still an indirect, nonspecific predictor.

RDW max in this window may reflect transfusion/ongoing inflammation and correlates with overall acuity, but it is not renal-specific. It provides at best a weak-to-moderate proxy signal for later creatinine rise.

A late RDW max could indicate persistent inflammatory/hematologic stress and transfusion exposure, which can track with AKI risk but indirectly. Despite better timing, it remains a nonspecific marker rather than a kidney/perfusion indicator.
RDW minimum is rarely clinically meaningful and is not tied to renal perfusion or injury mechanisms. It is likely redundant/noisy relative to mean/max and offers minimal predictive value.
RDW min is a low-information statistic with little physiologic interpretability for AKI prediction. Any association would be incidental and redundant with other RDW summaries.
Even in the closest window, RDW minimum does not capture a plausible renal pathway and is mostly noise/redundant. It is unlikely to improve sparse linear prediction of creatinine ratio.

Measurement frequency of RDW (via CBCs) is partly a monitoring-intensity proxy that can reflect instability/bleeding workup, indirectly related to AKI risk. As a _measured feature it is still not a physiologic renal marker and should be down-weighted.

More frequent CBC/RDW checks may indicate ongoing clinical concern (bleeding, transfusion, instability), which can correlate with AKI risk but nonspecifically. As a monitoring proxy, it should not score above 2.

RDW measurement frequency is mainly a monitoring/ordering-intensity signal rather than a renal mechanism. As a _measured feature it is a weak proxy for illness severity and should add little to predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Mean chloride early post-op reflects fluid/acid-base management (e.g., saline load) with only indirect links to AKI risk. The 0–12h mean can wash out brief hyperchloremia and is confounded by immediate resuscitation.

Chloride mean in 13–24h may reflect ongoing hyperchloremia from fluids and metabolic status, which has a modest association with renal vasoconstriction/AKI. Still indirect and the mean dilutes extremes.

Later mean chloride is closer to the outcome window and may capture sustained hyperchloremia/acid-base derangements from continued fluid strategy. Renal linkage remains indirect but timing improves prognostic value.

Peak chloride in the immediate post-op period can indicate a large chloride load and potential renal vasoconstrictive milieu, but early peaks are often transient and treatment-driven. Useful but weakly proximal to Hour-60 creatinine changes.

Peak chloride during 13–24h better reflects persistent hyperchloremia beyond the immediate bypass/resuscitation phase. Extremes are more informative than means for capturing potentially harmful exposure.
Peak chloride in 25–36h is temporally closer and may signal ongoing high-chloride fluid exposure or impaired clearance. Still not a direct kidney injury biomarker, so it remains a moderate predictor.
Low chloride early post-op is usually nonspecific (dilution, diuretics, lab variation) and not a well-established AKI pathway. The immediate post-op window further limits interpretability.
Minimum chloride is generally a weak and nonspecific marker with limited mechanistic linkage to AKI compared with hyperchloremia. Adds little beyond other acid-base/hemodynamic variables.
Even close to the outcome, low chloride is not a strong renal-specific signal and is often driven by dilutional states or therapy. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is likely minimal.

Number/frequency of chloride measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than physiology. _measured variables are poor mechanistic predictors of creatinine trajectory.
Chloride measurement frequency is a care-process proxy and not renal-specific. Any association with AKI would be indirect via overall instability and is better captured by physiologic values.

More frequent chloride checks late may indicate ongoing acid-base management or instability, which can correlate with AKI risk. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so it should not score high.

HDL measurement is unrelated to acute renal perfusion or injury and mostly reflects baseline lipid evaluation or ordering habits. As a measured/ordered indicator, it has minimal relevance to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
HDL being measured in ICU is a nonspecific care-process signal with no direct AKI pathway. It is unlikely to add predictive information for postoperative creatinine rise.
Late HDL measurement remains a non-renal, low-yield ordering proxy rather than a physiologic precursor of AKI. It should contribute little to predicting creatinine_ratio.

Mean neutrophil % reflects early postoperative inflammatory/stress response or infection risk, which is only indirectly related to AKI. In the immediate 0–12h window it is nonspecific and diluted by averaging.

Persistent elevation in neutrophil % may indicate ongoing inflammation/sepsis risk that can contribute to AKI, but remains an indirect proxy. Mean aggregation and mid-epoch timing limit specificity for Hour-60 creatinine rise.
Peak neutrophil % captures acute stress/inflammatory surge after surgery/CPB, which is weakly associated with later AKI. Early postoperative extremes are common and not kidney-specific.

A high peak neutrophil % in 13–24h may better reflect evolving infection/inflammation than 0–12h, but still lacks renal specificity. It is a general severity signal rather than a direct AKI pathway marker.
Low neutrophil % early post-op is uncommon and usually reflects lab variability or dilution rather than a renal mechanism. Minimal prognostic proximity to creatinine ratio at 60h.
Minimum neutrophil % is not a meaningful renal perfusion or injury marker and is often noise/measurement variability. Weak and nonspecific for predicting later creatinine rise.
Measurement frequency mainly reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern rather than physiology. Not kidney-specific and heavily subject to care-process proxy penalty.
How often neutrophil % is checked is a workflow/acuity proxy and not a direct AKI signal. Does not reliably encode renal perfusion or injury risk.

Later measurement frequency may reflect escalating concern for infection/sepsis closer to the outcome window, which can secondarily worsen kidney function. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Albumin reflects oncotic pressure, capillary leak/inflammation, and nutritional reserve; low levels correlate with AKI risk and fluid shifts. However in 0–12h it is strongly influenced by hemodilution/resuscitation, making it a moderate (not top-tier) predictor.

Maximum albumin in the immediate post-op period is less informative for AKI than low values and is affected by transfusion/volume status. Limited renal specificity and weaker prognostic directionality.
Minimum albumin better captures hemodilution/capillary leak and low oncotic state associated with worse outcomes and AKI risk. Still early and nonspecific, so moderate importance.
Albumin measurement frequency is largely a clinician-ordering/monitoring proxy (nutrition, liver function, volume status) rather than a renal mechanism. High proxy penalty and low renal specificity.
How often albumin is checked in 13–24h reflects care patterns and perceived illness severity more than kidney injury biology. Not a direct predictor of creatinine ratio.

Closer to Hour 60, repeated albumin checks may indicate ongoing capillary leak, large fluid shifts, or clinical deterioration that can contribute to AKI. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so kept low.

Whether triglycerides were measured is a care-process indicator (e.g., propofol monitoring, nutrition) with minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion or injury. Very weak relevance to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Whether triglycerides were checked in 13–24h is mainly a monitoring/TPN/propofol-related practice signal rather than a renal mechanism. Measurement frequency has high proxy penalty and weak linkage to AKI/creatinine rise.

Triglyceride measurement in 25–36h reflects clinician behavior (e.g., propofol monitoring, nutrition) more than renal perfusion or injury. Even though temporally closer, it remains a weak, nonspecific proxy.

Mean hemoglobin early post-op reflects anemia/hemodilution and transfusion context, which can influence renal oxygen delivery but is indirect and often transient after bypass. Mean aggregation may wash out brief low-Hb episodes most relevant to renal hypoxia.

Average hemoglobin in 13–24h captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution and bleeding/transfusion balance, with modest relevance to renal oxygen delivery. Still indirect for AKI and mean values can miss clinically important nadirs.

Sustained anemia by 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome and may reflect ongoing bleeding, hemodilution, or inadequate oxygen delivery that can worsen renal injury. It remains an indirect predictor compared with direct renal/hemodynamic markers.

Peak hemoglobin in the immediate post-op window is usually driven by transfusion/hemoconcentration and is not a recognized AKI pathway. It has low renal specificity and high confounding by resuscitation practices.

Maximum hemoglobin in 13–24h mostly reflects transfusion targets and fluid shifts rather than renal perfusion failure or injury. Extremes on the high side are not strongly prognostic for creatinine rise.
High-end hemoglobin near 25–36h is more a marker of transfusion/diuresis/hemoconcentration than kidney injury. Limited renal specificity and substantial clinician-behavior confounding.

Early nadir hemoglobin can indicate significant anemia/hemodilution/bleeding, which may contribute to renal hypoxia and AKI risk, but immediate post-bypass lows are common and sometimes transient. Min aggregation is appropriate for capturing the insult, but timing is less predictive.

A low hemoglobin nadir in 13–24h suggests persistent anemia or ongoing bleeding/transfusion needs, which can worsen renal oxygen delivery and correlate with complications. Still an indirect AKI predictor but min captures the clinically relevant extreme.

A low hemoglobin nadir in 25–36h is a near-term signal of ongoing anemia/bleeding or inadequate resuscitation, plausibly contributing to renal hypoxia and worsening kidney function by Hour 60. Min aggregation captures the most pathologic exposure and is temporally close.

Frequency/occurrence of hemoglobin measurement early post-op reflects monitoring intensity, bleeding concern, or instability rather than kidney-specific physiology. Proxy penalty is high, but it can weakly tag high-acuity courses.

Hemoglobin measurement frequency in 13–24h is largely a care-process marker (bleeding/transfusion surveillance) with indirect association to AKI risk. Not a physiologic renal marker, so importance remains low.

Checking hemoglobin in 25–36h may indicate ongoing bleeding, transfusion, or instability closer to the outcome, but it is still primarily clinician behavior/monitoring intensity. Per rules, measured variables should stay low despite temporal proximity.

Whether TEG fibrinogen activity was measured early post-op reflects coagulation management practice and bleeding concern, not a direct renal injury pathway. High proxy penalty and weak specificity for creatinine rise.
TEG fibrinogen measurement in 13–24h is mainly a marker of ongoing bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and clinician behavior. It has minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion or evolving AKI.

Indicates whether TEG fibrinogen activity was checked, largely reflecting bleeding/coagulation evaluation and monitoring intensity. It has minimal direct renal mechanistic linkage and is a care-process proxy, so it is weak for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

ACT reflects anticoagulation/heparin reversal and CPB-related coagulation management rather than kidney function. Early postoperative mean values are often transient and indirect for AKI risk, so predictive value for creatinine ratio is limited.

Peak ACT may capture excessive anticoagulation or prolonged CPB/heparin effect, which can correlate with operative complexity and bleeding. This is still an indirect proxy for renal injury and occurs in the immediate post-op window, so it is a weak predictor.
Low ACT mainly reflects heparin reversal/normalization and is not a renal-specific pathway. The minimum in the immediate post-op period is unlikely to add meaningful signal for later creatinine rise.

Measurement frequency of ACT primarily reflects monitoring intensity and procedural context (e.g., CPB/bleeding concerns). As a clinician-behavior proxy with little renal specificity, it should contribute minimally.

ACT being measured in this window suggests ongoing anticoagulation/bleeding evaluation rather than kidney injury. It is mostly a care-process signal with weak mechanistic connection to creatinine trajectory.

Late ACT measurement may indicate ongoing instability (bleeding, anticoagulation, ECMO/IABP context) which can correlate with AKI risk via shock/transfusion burden. Still, it is primarily monitoring-intensity and not renal-specific, so only modest importance.

AST reflects hepatic/ischemic injury or hemolysis and can correlate with global hypoperfusion after surgery. In the immediate post-op period the mean may dilute brief insults and is indirect for renal injury, so it is a weak-to-moderate predictor.

Peak AST early after surgery can signal significant ischemic hepatitis or hemolysis, serving as a marker of severe systemic hypoperfusion/inflammation that increases AKI risk. Although indirect and early, the extreme value better captures clinically meaningful insult.
Low AST has little clinical meaning for renal perfusion or AKI pathways and is often just baseline/assay noise. The minimum provides minimal prognostic information for creatinine ratio.

Whether/how often AST was checked mainly reflects lab-ordering intensity and concern for hepatic injury rather than kidney-specific physiology. As a monitoring proxy, it should have minimal predictive value.

AST measurement in this window is still largely a clinician-suspicion/monitoring signal for hepatic injury or hemolysis. It is not renal-specific and should not strongly predict Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Late AST measurement may indicate ongoing systemic instability or suspected ischemic injury, which can co-occur with evolving AKI. However it remains a measurement-intensity proxy, so importance is capped at low-moderate.

Total protein reflects hemodilution, capillary leak, and nutritional/inflammatory status, which can relate indirectly to fluid balance and renal congestion. Early postoperative mean values are nonspecific and heavily confounded by resuscitation, so predictive value is limited.
Higher total protein early post-op is usually not a meaningful renal-risk signal and may reflect less hemodilution rather than pathology. It is nonspecific and unlikely to predict creatinine ratio.

Low total protein can indicate marked hemodilution/capillary leak and large-volume resuscitation, which may associate with renal congestion and AKI risk. Still indirect and early, so it is only a weak-to-moderate predictor.
Measurement frequency of total protein is mainly a monitoring-intensity/care-process proxy and not kidney-specific. Early postop timing further limits relevance to Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Total protein measurement count reflects clinician behavior rather than renal physiology. Even in the trajectory phase it is not a specific AKI pathway signal.

Late-window measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving instability or concern (e.g., nutrition/oncotic status workup), but remains nonspecific and largely process-driven. Not a direct renal biomarker, so capped low.

Whether HbA1c was checked is an indirect proxy for diabetes/renal reserve assessment rather than an acute renal insult marker. In the immediate postop window it adds limited incremental information beyond known comorbidity.

HbA1c measurement indicates chronic glycemic risk/diabetes evaluation, which is only a moderate baseline AKI risk factor and not temporally proximal. Mostly reflects ordering behavior rather than kidney injury evolution.
Late HbA1c ordering still represents chronic risk profiling and clinician behavior, not a near-term physiologic precursor of AKI. Thus it is a weak predictor for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Mean serum CO2 (bicarbonate) reflects acid-base status, which can correlate with shock/renal dysfunction but is nonspecific early after bypass and resuscitation. Mean aggregation may wash out brief but important derangements.

Persistently low/abnormal bicarbonate in this window can indicate ongoing metabolic acidosis from hypoperfusion or impaired renal acid handling, which relates to AKI risk. Still indirect and mean-based, so moderate importance.

Sustained bicarbonate abnormalities closer to the outcome can signal ongoing hypoperfusion, sepsis, or reduced renal function, making it a moderate predictor. Mean captures sustained exposure but remains nonspecific for kidney injury.

High CO2/bicarbonate early postop often reflects ventilation/alkalosis or transfusion/diuretic effects and is not kidney-specific. Early transient extremes are common and weakly tied to later creatinine rise.
Elevated bicarbonate may reflect contraction alkalosis/diuretic use or ventilatory management, which is only indirectly related to renal outcomes. Max is less informative for AKI than low extremes.

Late high bicarbonate can still be driven by diuresis or ventilation rather than renal injury, so renal specificity is limited. Max aggregation is not the key pathology signal for AKI compared with low CO2.

Low minimum CO2 captures metabolic acidosis episodes that can reflect hypoperfusion/shock, a plausible AKI precursor, but early postop acidosis is common and may be transient. Min is the most relevant aggregation within the CO2 family.

A low CO2 nadir in the trajectory phase suggests ongoing metabolic acidosis from persistent hypoperfusion or impaired renal acid excretion, both linked to subsequent creatinine rise. Min aggregation captures clinically meaningful insults better than mean/max.

Low bicarbonate closest to Hour 60 is a strong leading indicator of ongoing shock/sepsis or evolving renal dysfunction, which increases risk of rising creatinine. The minimum value best preserves the signal of clinically important acidosis.

Frequency of CO2 checks mainly reflects monitoring intensity and perceived instability rather than a renal mechanism. In the immediate postop period it may weakly proxy acuity but is not kidney-specific.

Frequency of CO2 (likely BMP total CO2/bicarbonate) checks mainly reflects monitoring intensity rather than renal physiology. At best an indirect proxy for acid–base concern, so low renal specificity and high proxy penalty.

More frequent CO2/bicarbonate measurement in the 25–36h window may signal evolving metabolic acidosis or renal concern closer to the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. Still primarily a clinician-behavior/acuity proxy, so capped low.

Whether BNP was measured early post-op mostly indicates clinician concern for heart failure/volume status rather than a kidney-specific mechanism. Immediate post-op ordering is common and nonspecific, so minimal direct value for creatinine ratio.

BNP measurement in the trajectory phase may reflect persistent congestion/low-output concern that can contribute to cardiorenal AKI. However it is still a measurement-frequency proxy rather than the BNP value, so weak predictor.

Late BNP ordering can mark ongoing hemodynamic/volume-status problems closer to the outcome window, which can relate to renal perfusion and venous congestion. Nonetheless it remains a clinician-intensity signal and not a direct renal biomarker.

Mean PaCO2 in the immediate post-op period largely reflects ventilator management/anesthesia effects and is not kidney-specific. Any renal link via acid–base is indirect and early values are often transient.

Sustained hypercapnia/hypoventilation over 13–24h can contribute to acidemia and hemodynamic stress, indirectly affecting renal perfusion. Still nonspecific and mean aggregation can wash out short clinically relevant extremes.

Mean PaCO2 closer to Hour 60 may reflect ongoing respiratory failure and acid–base burden that can worsen renal outcomes indirectly. Renal specificity remains low and mean is less informative than extremes for acute insults.

Peak PaCO2 early post-op may capture episodes of hypoventilation and acidemia, which can worsen hemodynamics and renal perfusion indirectly. However early post-op derangements are common/transient, limiting prognostic proximity.

A high PaCO2 peak in the 13–24h window suggests significant respiratory failure/acidemia that may contribute to renal hypoperfusion risk. Still an indirect pathway and not a primary AKI driver compared with hemodynamics/renal labs.

Peak PaCO2 in the 25–36h leading-indicator window can mark ongoing severe hypercapnia/acidemia near the outcome, which may worsen renal perfusion and vasopressor needs. Extreme aggregation is appropriate, but renal linkage remains indirect so not top-tier.
Low PaCO2 early post-op often reflects ventilator settings/hyperventilation rather than renal pathophysiology. It has weak and nonspecific association with subsequent creatinine rise.
Minimum PaCO2 in this window is usually a ventilation-management artifact and not a recognized AKI pathway. Limited renal specificity and unclear directionality for creatinine ratio prediction.

Low PaCO2 near the outcome window still primarily reflects ventilatory strategy rather than kidney injury or perfusion. Minimal mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise compared with hypotension/oliguria/renal labs.
Number of PaCO2 measurements mainly captures monitoring intensity (e.g., frequent ABGs) rather than renal physiology. Early post-op ABG frequency is routine and nonspecific, so low usefulness.

More frequent PaCO2 checks in 13–24h may indicate persistent respiratory/hemodynamic instability that can secondarily affect renal outcomes. Still a clinician-behavior proxy and therefore limited predictive value.

Measurement frequency of PaCO2 reflects monitoring intensity/ventilation management rather than renal physiology. As a _measured proxy variable it has high clinician-behavior signal and minimal direct linkage to creatinine rise.

Whether indirect bilirubin was checked early post-op mainly reflects lab-ordering/acuity rather than kidney-specific injury. It is a weak, nonspecific proxy and the 0–12h window is prone to transient post-bypass derangements.

Indirect bilirubin measurement indicates clinician concern for hemolysis/hepatic dysfunction but is not kidney-specific. As a _measured feature it is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy with limited incremental value for predicting creatinine ratio.

Late measurement may correlate with ongoing instability/hemolysis that can contribute to AKI risk, but it still mostly captures clinician behavior rather than a renal mechanism. Per rules, _measured variables remain low importance despite closer timing.

Mean calcium in the immediate post-op period reflects transfusion/citrate load, albumin shifts, and resuscitation rather than direct renal injury. The mean can wash out clinically relevant extremes and early abnormalities are often transient.

Calcium mean in 13–24h may reflect ongoing critical illness and metabolic status but is an indirect AKI predictor. Mean aggregation is less sensitive to short hypocalcemic episodes and renal specificity is low.

Later calcium mean is closer to the outcome window but remains a nonspecific metabolic marker with weak mechanistic linkage to creatinine rise. Sustained mean calcium is generally less informative than extremes for acute insults.

Peak calcium early post-op can reflect calcium administration during resuscitation and is not kidney-specific. In 0–12h it is heavily influenced by peri-bypass practice patterns and has limited prognostic proximity to Hour 60 creatinine.

A higher calcium maximum may indicate supplementation or metabolic shifts but is an indirect and nonspecific correlate of illness severity. It has low renal specificity and is likely redundant with other acuity/hemodynamic variables.

Late peak calcium could reflect ongoing replacement or dysregulation but does not directly track renal perfusion or tubular injury. Despite better temporal proximity, its renal relevance remains weak.

Nadir calcium early post-op can capture citrate-related hypocalcemia from transfusion and severe physiologic stress, which may accompany shock states relevant to AKI. The extreme (min) is more informative than mean, but early timing limits specificity.

Low calcium in 13–24h may signal persistent critical illness, ongoing transfusion effects, or endocrine/metabolic derangement that can track with AKI risk. The minimum captures clinically meaningful extremes better than the mean, with moderate prognostic value.

A low calcium nadir in 25–36h may reflect ongoing instability and metabolic derangements closer to the Hour 60 creatinine outcome. Still, it is not a direct renal biomarker, so it remains a moderate (not high) predictor.

Frequency/occurrence of calcium measurement is primarily a monitoring and treatment-intensity signal rather than renal pathophysiology. As a _measured feature in the immediate post-op window it has high proxy penalty.
Calcium being checked in 13–24h reflects clinician concern and ICU practice patterns more than kidney injury mechanisms. Per rules, _measured variables are low-importance predictors.

Late calcium measurement may indicate ongoing instability or electrolyte management in sicker patients, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it remains largely a clinician-behavior proxy, so importance stays low.

Mean PTT in the immediate postop window reflects anticoagulation effect/consumption and bleeding risk more than renal physiology. It is an indirect severity/procedure proxy with limited specificity for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Later mean PTT may reflect ongoing heparin exposure, liver dysfunction, or consumptive coagulopathy, which can correlate with critical illness. Renal linkage is indirect and largely a proxy for complexity/instability rather than a kidney-specific pathway.

Peak early PTT often captures intra/postoperative heparinization or coagulopathy immediately after bypass. This is temporally distant and nonspecific for AKI, so predictive value for creatinine ratio is likely weak.

A high PTT close to the outcome can indicate persistent anticoagulation or systemic illness (e.g., hepatic dysfunction/DIC) that may accompany AKI. Still, it is not a direct renal marker and is confounded by clinician management, limiting importance.
Low/normal PTT early postop is usually not a meaningful renal-risk signal and may reflect baseline coagulation status. It has minimal mechanistic connection to evolving kidney injury.

Minimum PTT in the 25–36h window mainly reflects periods of normal coagulation rather than a pathologic insult relevant to AKI. It provides little kidney-specific prognostic information for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

PTT measurement frequency is primarily a monitoring-intensity/anticoagulation-management signal (e.g., heparin titration) rather than physiology. As a care-process proxy, it has low direct relevance to creatinine ratio.
Repeated PTT checks in 13–24h mostly indicate ongoing anticoagulation monitoring or concern for bleeding/thrombosis. This is a clinician-behavior proxy with weak renal specificity.

Frequent PTT measurement late (25–36h) may signal persistent instability, anticoagulation needs, or complications that can co-occur with AKI. Still, it is largely a monitoring proxy, so it should not score high.
Mean monocyte count early postop reflects nonspecific inflammatory response to surgery/CPB and hemodilution. It is not kidney-specific and is a weak predictor of subsequent creatinine rise.

Monocyte mean in 13–24h remains a nonspecific inflammation marker with substantial noise and limited mechanistic linkage to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is likely minimal.

Peak monocyte count in the first 12h is dominated by postoperative inflammatory shifts and lab variability. It lacks renal specificity and is unlikely to add meaningful signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.
A higher monocyte peak in 13–24h may reflect systemic inflammation/infection risk but is still an indirect and weak AKI predictor. It is not a proximate renal biomarker and is prone to confounding.
Low monocyte counts early are often due to dilutional effects or stress leukogram patterns rather than renal injury. Minimum values are not a meaningful AKI pathway indicator.
Minimum monocyte count in 13–24h is nonspecific and does not capture a renal insult or perfusion failure. It is unlikely to contribute to predicting creatinine ratio.
Measurement count reflects lab ordering frequency (monitoring intensity) rather than a physiologic renal mechanism. As a proxy variable, it has low expected usefulness for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Count of monocyte labs obtained in 13–24h; this is primarily a monitoring-intensity/clinician-behavior signal. It has no direct renal specificity and is an indirect proxy at best, so predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is minimal.

Frequency of monocyte count measurement in 25–36h reflects lab ordering intensity rather than kidney physiology. Even though temporally closer to Hour 60, it remains a weak, nonspecific proxy and should not drive a sparse renal model.

Mean phosphorus early post-op can reflect baseline CKD, cell shifts, transfusion/hemolysis, or early renal handling changes. In the immediate post-bypass period it is often transient and the mean can wash out extremes, limiting specificity for evolving AKI.

Average phosphorus in 13–24h may weakly track renal clearance and catabolic state, but remains nonspecific and influenced by nutrition/repletion practices. Mean aggregation reduces sensitivity to clinically meaningful peaks related to AKI.

Sustained higher phosphorus closer to the outcome window can indicate reduced renal excretion and evolving kidney dysfunction. Still moderately nonspecific (affected by supplementation and shifts), so it is a moderate rather than top-tier predictor.

Peak phosphorus in 0–12h could reflect early renal hypoperfusion or tissue breakdown, but immediate post-op shifts and resuscitation effects are common. The max captures extremes, yet timing makes it a weaker predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

A higher phosphorus peak in 13–24h is more suggestive of impaired renal clearance or ongoing catabolism than in the first 12h. Max aggregation captures clinically relevant derangements, giving moderate prognostic value for later creatinine rise.

Peak phosphorus in 25–36h is a relatively proximal signal of reduced renal excretion and evolving AKI physiology. The extreme value is clinically meaningful and temporally close to Hour 60, making it a strong predictor.

Low phosphorus early post-op is commonly driven by dilution, insulin/catecholamine shifts, or refeeding effects rather than kidney injury. The minimum is not a typical AKI pathway marker and has low specificity for later creatinine increase.

A low phosphorus nadir in 13–24h more often reflects intracellular shifts or replacement practices than impaired renal function. It has weak mechanistic linkage to AKI and limited value for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Low phosphorus in 25–36h is still more consistent with supplementation/shift phenomena than renal failure (which tends to raise phosphorus). As a minimum, it is poorly aligned with AKI mechanisms and thus low utility.
Number of phosphorus measurements in 0–12h mainly reflects ICU lab-ordering patterns and immediate post-op protocols. It is a process/monitoring proxy with little renal specificity.

Measurement frequency of phosphorus in 13–24h is largely clinician behavior and care intensity rather than a physiologic renal marker. Proxy penalty is high and it should contribute little to predicting creatinine ratio.

More frequent phosphorus checks in 25–36h may indicate clinician concern for renal dysfunction or electrolyte derangements, making it a slightly informative proxy. However it remains monitoring-intensity driven and should not score above 2.

Mean neutrophil count reflects postoperative inflammation/stress response and possible infection risk, which can contribute indirectly to AKI. In 0–12h it is nonspecific and heavily influenced by surgery/CPB, limiting renal prognostic proximity.

Average neutrophil count in 13–24h may better reflect evolving inflammatory burden than the immediate post-op period, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still lacks renal specificity and is a moderate-to-weak predictor on its own.

Peak neutrophil count reflects early postoperative inflammation/stress response. It is only indirectly linked to AKI (systemic inflammation) and is in the immediate post-op window where transient changes are common.
Peak neutrophil count in the trajectory phase may indicate ongoing inflammation/infection risk. Renal specificity is low and it remains an indirect predictor of creatinine rise.

Lowest neutrophil count early post-op is usually nonspecific (hemodilution/lab variability) and not mechanistically tied to renal perfusion or tubular injury. Limited prognostic proximity to Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Minimum neutrophil count is a weak, nonspecific signal and does not directly reflect kidney function or AKI pathways. Adds little beyond general illness context.
Measurement count mainly reflects monitoring intensity and routine post-cardiac surgery lab schedules. It is a care-process proxy with minimal renal mechanistic information.
Frequency of ANC checks is largely driven by ICU protocols or clinician concern rather than kidney physiology. Proxy penalty is high and renal specificity is low.
More frequent ANC measurement closer to the outcome may reflect evolving complications (infection/sepsis) that can contribute to AKI. Still primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy, so capped low.

Troponin measurement frequency is mostly protocolized after cardiac surgery and reflects monitoring rather than myocardial injury magnitude. Renal relevance is indirect (hemodynamics) and the variable is a process proxy.
Counting troponin checks mainly captures clinician behavior and surveillance intensity. It does not directly encode renal perfusion or kidney injury and is not a strong predictor of creatinine ratio.
Later troponin measurement frequency may signal concern for ongoing myocardial ischemia/low output that could impair renal perfusion. However it remains a monitoring proxy and cannot score high.

Mean PaO2 reflects oxygenation/ventilation status early post-op, which can correlate with overall instability. Renal specificity is low and early averages may wash out brief hypoxemia relevant to AKI risk.

Sustained oxygenation in the 13–24h window may reflect recovery vs ongoing respiratory failure and illness severity. Association with AKI is indirect and mean aggregation is less sensitive to short insults.

Mean PaO2 closer to Hour 60 can capture persistent respiratory failure/ARDS and ongoing critical illness that increases AKI risk via hypoxemia and hemodynamic interactions. Still indirect for renal injury, so moderate score.
Maximum PaO2 is often driven by FiO2 settings and hyperoxia rather than pathology, making it a clinician-management proxy. It has weak and nonspecific linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.
Peak PaO2 largely reflects ventilator/oxygen titration and does not capture hypoxemic insults relevant to renal hypoperfusion. Low renal specificity and high proxy penalty.

Even near the outcome, maximum PaO2 is dominated by oxygen delivery settings and is not a strong physiologic precursor of AKI. Provides little incremental renal-relevant signal compared with measures of hypoxemia.

Lowest PaO2 in the immediate postop window reflects hypoxemia/respiratory failure and overall physiologic stress, which can contribute indirectly to renal ischemia. Early transient hypoxemia is common after cardiac surgery and is a nonspecific proxy, so predictive value for Hour-60 creatinine ratio is limited.

Minimum PaO2 during 13–24h captures persistent hypoxemia beyond the immediate postop period, which may correlate with ongoing shock/low delivery and AKI risk. Still indirect and nonspecific for kidney injury, so only weak-to-moderate usefulness.

Minimum PaO2 in the 25–36h leading-indicator window reflects ongoing impaired oxygenation closer to the creatinine outcome and may signal sustained low oxygen delivery contributing to renal hypoperfusion. It remains an indirect marker (not renal-specific), but timing and extreme aggregation increase relevance.

Count/frequency of PaO2 measurements mainly reflects monitoring intensity (ventilation changes, instability, clinician concern) rather than a renal mechanism. Strong proxy penalty and early postop context make it minimally informative for creatinine ratio.

PaO2 measurement frequency in 13–24h is largely a care-process/acuity marker (ABG checks) rather than kidney-specific physiology. Proxy penalty dominates, so it adds little direct predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

More frequent PaO2 checks in 25–36h may indicate ongoing respiratory/hemodynamic instability closer to the outcome, which can co-occur with AKI risk. Still primarily monitoring intensity rather than renal pathophysiology, so capped at low importance.

Mean potassium early after surgery reflects a mix of shifts (acidosis, transfusion, insulin), renal handling, and treatment effects. Because it is averaged and early, it is a weak and somewhat treatment-confounded indicator of evolving renal dysfunction.

Mean potassium in 13–24h may begin to reflect impaired renal excretion or ongoing tissue breakdown, but is still influenced by supplementation/diuretics/insulin. Mean aggregation can wash out clinically important peaks, limiting predictive strength.

Mean potassium in 25–36h is closer to the Hour-60 creatinine and may reflect sustained reduced renal clearance or ongoing catabolic/acidemic state. Still not a direct kidney biomarker and is treatment-sensitive, but timing supports moderate importance.

Peak potassium early can signal reduced renal excretion, hemolysis/rhabdo, acidosis, or massive transfusion—pathways associated with AKI risk. However, immediate postop peaks are often transient and treatment-related, so not top-tier.

Maximum potassium in 13–24h better captures persistent hyperkalemia beyond the immediate postop period, which can reflect declining renal function and severity of illness. Still confounded by supplementation and therapies, keeping it moderate.

Maximum potassium in 25–36h is a strong near-term signal that may reflect impaired renal clearance as AKI evolves, and extremes are clinically meaningful. Close timing to the outcome and use of peak value increase prognostic proximity despite some treatment confounding.

Lowest potassium early is more likely driven by diuretics, insulin, alkalosis, or replacement practices than intrinsic renal injury. It is weakly related to creatinine rise and heavily confounded in the immediate postop period.

Minimum potassium in 13–24h remains largely a marker of treatment effects (diuresis, insulin, repletion strategy) rather than renal dysfunction. Limited renal specificity and indirect linkage to creatinine ratio.

Low potassium in 25–36h can reflect ongoing diuretic use or aggressive replacement patterns and is not a typical mechanistic precursor of creatinine rise. Despite closer timing, renal specificity is low and proxy/treatment confounding is high.

Potassium measurement frequency primarily reflects monitoring intensity and clinician concern (e.g., arrhythmia risk, electrolyte management) rather than kidney injury itself. Strong proxy penalty and early postop context make it minimally predictive for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Counts how often potassium was checked in the 13–24h window, reflecting monitoring intensity and clinician concern. It is an indirect proxy for instability/renal dysfunction and should not be weighted highly versus physiologic potassium values.
Frequency of potassium measurement in the 25–36h window, mainly a care-process/acuity signal rather than a renal mechanism. Slightly more temporally proximate to Hour 60 but still a proxy variable.

Average bilirubin early post-op reflects hepatic congestion/hemolysis and overall shock burden more than kidney-specific injury. Mean in 0–12h can wash out transient peaks and is temporally distant from the Hour 60 creatinine ratio.

Peak bilirubin in the immediate post-op period may capture early hypoperfusion/hemolysis after bypass, which correlates with multi-organ dysfunction. It is still indirect for AKI and early transient elevations are common, limiting predictive specificity.

Minimum bilirubin in 0–12h is rarely informative for renal outcomes and mostly reflects baseline/measurement noise. It has low renal specificity and poor mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.
Number of bilirubin checks early post-op is primarily a monitoring/clinician-behavior proxy. It has minimal direct relationship to renal perfusion or evolving AKI.

Measurement frequency of bilirubin in 13–24h reflects concern for hepatic dysfunction or hemolysis rather than kidney injury. As a process variable, it adds little specific signal for Hour 60 creatinine ratio.
Bilirubin measurement frequency in 25–36h is still mainly a proxy for illness severity/workup intensity. Despite closer timing, it lacks renal specificity and should not be up-weighted.

Mean RBC count early post-op reflects anemia/hemodilution/transfusion context, which can influence renal oxygen delivery indirectly. In the immediate 0–12h window it is confounded by bypass-related dilution and resuscitation, limiting specificity for AKI.

Average RBC count in 13–24h captures ongoing anemia/hemodilution that may correlate with overall recovery and oxygen delivery. It remains an indirect predictor for creatinine rise and is less informative than hemodynamics or direct renal markers.
Mean RBC count in 25–36h reflects sustained anemia which can contribute to renal hypoxia but is not kidney-specific. Closer temporal proximity helps, yet it remains a moderate, indirect signal.

Maximum RBC count in 0–12h is usually driven by transfusion timing or hemoconcentration and is not a clear AKI pathway marker. It is poorly specific and highly confounded in the immediate post-op period.
Peak RBC count in 13–24h mainly reflects transfusion/volume status rather than renal injury. As an extreme in the high direction, it has weak mechanistic linkage to worsening creatinine.
Maximum RBC count in 25–36h is typically a transfusion/hemoconcentration artifact and not a strong renal predictor. It adds little beyond anemia minima/means and has low renal specificity.

Minimum RBC count early post-op captures nadir anemia/hemodilution and potential bleeding/transfusion needs, which can impair renal oxygen delivery and correlate with severity. The min is the most clinically relevant aggregation for anemia, though early timing is still confounded.

Minimum RBC count in 13–24h reflects persistent or worsening anemia after initial resuscitation, a more informative trajectory-phase signal. It is an indirect but established risk context for AKI via reduced oxygen delivery and overall illness severity.

Low RBC count reflects anemia/hemodilution or bleeding/transfusion burden, which can correlate with overall illness and oxygen delivery but is not kidney-specific. The 25–36h minimum may capture persistent anemia, yet renal linkage is indirect and confounded by transfusion/resuscitation.

Indicates that RBC count was checked in the immediate postoperative period, mainly reflecting monitoring intensity and routine post-op labs. It has minimal direct mechanistic linkage to subsequent creatinine rise.
Lab measurement frequency is largely a care-process proxy rather than a renal physiology signal. At 13–24h it may reflect acuity, but remains nonspecific for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Having RBC measured closer to the outcome can reflect ongoing instability/bleeding or clinician concern, which may correlate with AKI risk. Still, it is primarily a monitoring-intensity proxy and not kidney-specific.
D-dimer being measured early is mostly a clinician-suspicion/diagnostic workup marker (thrombosis/bleeding) rather than a direct AKI pathway. Immediate post-op testing is common and nonspecific.
Measurement of D-dimer reflects diagnostic intensity and concern for thromboembolism or coagulopathy, not renal function. Any association with AKI would be indirect via severity of illness.

D-dimer measurement in the 25–36h window may signal evolving complications (thrombosis, inflammation, DIC) that can secondarily affect renal perfusion. However it remains a workup/behavior proxy and not kidney-specific.
TEG ACT being measured early mainly indicates perioperative coagulation monitoring and transfusion management. It is not a direct marker of renal injury and is heavily influenced by care processes.
Ongoing TEG ACT measurement suggests continued bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation, which is nonspecific for AKI prediction. The signal is largely clinician-driven rather than renal-mechanistic.

Late TEG monitoring can indicate persistent coagulopathy/bleeding requiring transfusion and hemodynamic support, which can contribute indirectly to AKI risk. Still, it is primarily a monitoring/procedure proxy.

Immature granulocytes reflect systemic inflammation/stress response, which can be associated with postoperative complications and AKI risk but is nonspecific. The early (0–12h) mean is prone to transient post-bypass inflammatory effects.

Persistent elevation suggests ongoing inflammation/infection risk, which can contribute to AKI via sepsis-like physiology, but remains indirect. Mean aggregation may dilute short spikes and is less informative than extremes.

Peak immature granulocytes capture the strongest inflammatory signal, which can correlate with later organ dysfunction including AKI, but is not kidney-specific. In the immediate post-op window, peaks are common and less prognostically specific.

A high peak at 13–24h may indicate evolving infection or sustained inflammatory response beyond expected post-bypass changes, which is a moderate AKI risk factor. Max aggregation better captures clinically meaningful inflammatory surges than the mean.

The minimum immature granulocyte percentage is not clinically meaningful for AKI prediction and mainly reflects baseline/measurement noise. It has poor renal specificity and weak prognostic proximity.
A low minimum value provides little information about inflammatory burden or impending renal injury. It is a weak, non-mechanistic signal compared with max-based summaries.

Measurement frequency of immature granulocyte % is mainly a monitoring-intensity/sepsis-workup proxy, not a renal mechanism. Early postop timing and “_measured” aggregation add strong proxy penalty with little direct AKI signal.
Counts how often immature granulocyte % was checked, reflecting clinician concern rather than kidney function. Still indirect for AKI and “_measured” features should be low importance.

Later measurement frequency may weakly proxy evolving infection/inflammation that can contribute to AKI, but it remains a care-process signal. Despite closer timing, “_measured” limits usefulness for predicting creatinine ratio.

Whether TEG fibrinolysis was measured mainly reflects bleeding/coagulopathy evaluation and monitoring intensity. It has no direct renal specificity and early postop ordering is common and nonspecific.

TEG fibrinolysis measurement indicates ongoing coagulation/bleeding assessment rather than renal perfusion or injury. As a measured/ordered flag, it is largely clinician-behavior driven with weak AKI linkage.

Late TEG measurement can proxy persistent bleeding/transfusion needs and systemic instability that may secondarily affect kidneys. Still primarily a process-of-care marker with limited renal specificity.

Mean early postoperative creatinine ratio reflects immediate renal function change after surgery/CPB. Useful but discounted because early transient hemodilution/resuscitation can blur true injury and mean can wash out peaks.
Creatinine ratio in the 13–24h trajectory phase captures early AKI evolution vs recovery. Mean is informative but slightly less than extremes for detecting evolving injury.

Creatinine ratio close to the outcome window is a direct kidney function biomarker and strong leading indicator of the Hour 60 ratio. Mean over 25–36h still closely tracks evolving AKI in this timeframe.

Peak creatinine ratio early may capture an early rise suggesting renal insult, but immediate postop values can be confounded by fluid shifts and lab timing. Max is better than mean for detecting early injury yet still temporally discounted.
Peak creatinine ratio during 13–24h is a direct marker of evolving AKI and often precedes continued rise by Hour 60. The max aggregation captures clinically meaningful worsening better than the mean.

Peak creatinine ratio in 25–36h is a direct, near-term indicator of ongoing kidney injury and is highly prognostic for the Hour 60 ratio. Max captures the worst renal dysfunction signal closest to the target.

Minimum early creatinine ratio is often driven by hemodilution or measurement timing rather than renal recovery, reducing renal specificity. It is somewhat informative for baseline drift but less predictive than max/mean for AKI.

Minimum creatinine ratio in this window may reflect transient dilution or partial recovery, but it is less aligned with AKI progression than peaks. Redundant with mean/max and typically weaker for predicting later elevation.

Minimum creatinine ratio close to the outcome can still indicate whether renal function ever returns toward baseline in the leading-indicator window. However, it is generally less predictive than max for impending worsening.

Frequency of creatinine measurement reflects clinician concern/ICU protocol intensity more than renal physiology. It may weakly correlate with instability/AKI suspicion but is a proxy variable and early postop labs are routine.

Counts whether creatinine ratio was measured in the 13–24h window, reflecting monitoring intensity/clinician concern rather than renal physiology. May weakly proxy evolving instability/AKI risk but is indirect and redundant with actual creatinine values.

Measurement indicator closest to the Hour-60 outcome; may reflect heightened concern for renal dysfunction or hemodynamic instability. Still a care-process proxy (not a biomarker), so capped low despite temporal proximity.

Use of hyperoncotic 25% albumin early post-op often signals hypotension/vasoplegia, capillary leak, or attempts to augment intravascular volume. Renal link is indirect and early window is noisy; substantial clinician-behavior proxy.

25% albumin use in the trajectory phase may indicate ongoing third-spacing or hemodynamic support needs that can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and treatment-driven, so only weak-to-moderate predictive value.

Late 25% albumin use suggests persistent intravascular depletion/vasoplegia or aggressive fluid management close to the outcome window, which can correlate with evolving AKI risk. However it remains a clinician-response proxy and not kidney-specific.

5% albumin volume expansion early post-op reflects resuscitation needs and possible low effective circulating volume. Renal relevance is indirect and early postoperative dosing is common and confounded by routine practice.

Ongoing 5% albumin in 13–24h may indicate continued fluid responsiveness issues or capillary leak, which can associate with renal hypoperfusion. Still largely a management choice with modest specificity for AKI.

5% albumin use in 25–36h suggests persistent hemodynamic/volume problems nearer to Hour 60, which can precede creatinine rise. Predictive value is moderate but limited by strong treatment/proxy confounding.

Analgesic dosing primarily reflects pain control/sedation strategy and extubation trajectory. Not kidney-specific and unlikely to causally affect creatinine ratio at typical doses; strong proxy penalty.
Continued acetaminophen-codeine use reflects analgesia needs and care patterns rather than renal perfusion or injury. Minimal direct prognostic linkage to AKI/creatinine rise.
Late analgesic dosing is mainly a marker of ongoing pain management and functional recovery. Little mechanistic connection to renal injury and high clinician-behavior confounding.
Acetaminophen dosing is routine postoperative analgesia/antipyresis and generally not nephrotoxic at standard dosing. Provides minimal information about renal function or impending AKI.
Represents routine medication administration and does not track renal perfusion or injury pathways. Any association with creatinine ratio would be nonspecific and weak.
Late acetaminophen use remains a nonspecific care-process variable with negligible renal specificity. Unlikely to add predictive signal for Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Allopurinol use reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia and may correlate with baseline CKD/renal reserve, but perioperative dosing itself is not an AKI biomarker. Early window and medication-choice confounding limit usefulness.

Continuation of allopurinol suggests pre-existing comorbidity (often linked with CKD) rather than acute renal physiology. Modest baseline-risk signal but indirect for predicting Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Allopurinol use/dose mainly reflects chronic gout/hyperuricemia management rather than an acute renal perfusion or injury signal. In the 25–36h window it is still largely a care-process/chronic comorbidity proxy with weak linkage to Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Aminocaproic acid is an antifibrinolytic indicating bleeding risk/complex surgery and transfusion context, which can indirectly relate to AKI risk. In the immediate 0–12h period it is mostly a procedural severity proxy rather than a direct renal mechanism.

Ongoing aminocaproic acid suggests continued bleeding management or high-risk operative course, indirectly associated with renal injury via shock/transfusion/inflammation pathways. Still largely a proxy variable with limited renal specificity.

Late use may indicate persistent bleeding/instability closer to the outcome window, which can correlate with AKI risk. However it remains indirect and clinician-driven, not a kidney biomarker or perfusion measure.

Amiodarone dosing reflects postoperative atrial/ventricular arrhythmias and hemodynamic instability, which can secondarily affect renal perfusion. In 0–12h it is common and often transient, making it a weak-to-moderate proxy for AKI risk.

Continued amiodarone use suggests ongoing arrhythmia burden and possibly reduced cardiac output, indirectly relevant to renal perfusion. Renal specificity is low and it is partly treatment-pattern dependent.

Amiodarone requirement in the 25–36h window may mark persistent arrhythmias/low-output physiology closer to Hour 60, which can contribute to renal hypoperfusion. Still indirect and not specific for kidney injury, but timing increases prognostic value.

Amlodipine administration typically reflects resumption of chronic antihypertensive therapy rather than acute renal physiology. It has minimal direct linkage to evolving AKI and is heavily clinician-practice dependent early post-op.
Amlodipine use in this window mainly indicates stable blood pressure and medication reconciliation rather than renal injury pathways. Predictive value for creatinine ratio is likely minimal.

Late amlodipine dosing generally signals clinical stability and chronic hypertension management, not renal hypoperfusion or nephrotoxicity. Any association with creatinine change would be weak and indirect.

Ampicillin use reflects antibiotic selection/prophylaxis or suspected infection; it is not a classic nephrotoxin compared with alternatives. In 0–12h it is mostly a care-process marker with low renal specificity.

Ongoing ampicillin may indicate infection concern, which can relate to AKI via sepsis physiology, but the drug itself is not strongly nephrotoxic. Thus it is an indirect illness-severity proxy with modest relevance.

Antibiotic continuation closer to Hour 60 may capture evolving infection/inflammation that can worsen renal function. Still nonspecific and clinician-driven, with limited direct renal mechanistic signal.

Angiotensin II use indicates refractory vasodilatory shock requiring potent vasopressor support, a strong pathway to renal hypoperfusion/AKI. Even in 0–12h, needing this agent suggests severe hemodynamic instability with high prognostic relevance.

Angiotensin II requirement in 13–24h implies persistent shock and high vasopressor needs, strongly linked to subsequent AKI and creatinine rise. It is closer to the outcome and reflects sustained perfusion risk rather than transient post-bypass effects.

Angiotensin II use in 25–36h is a near-term marker of ongoing refractory shock and renal hypoperfusion immediately preceding the Hour-60 creatinine outcome. This is a very strong prognostic signal for worsening kidney function despite some clinician-behavior component.

Apixaban exposure early post-op mainly reflects anticoagulation strategy/AF history rather than renal physiology. Only weak, indirect linkage to creatinine change (proxy for comorbidity/bleeding risk), and early timing is less informative.

Apixaban dosing in this window is largely a care-process marker (anticoagulation choice) with minimal direct AKI pathway relevance. Any association with creatinine is indirect and confounded by clinician behavior.

Later apixaban use may weakly proxy clinical stability and renal function sufficient to resume anticoagulation, but it is not a renal biomarker. Temporal proximity is better, yet the signal is still mostly treatment-selection confounding.
Aspirin early after cardiac surgery reflects routine antiplatelet practice rather than kidney injury or perfusion. Not a meaningful mechanistic predictor of Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Aspirin administration is common and primarily protocol-driven, with little renal specificity. Any relationship to AKI is indirect and likely dominated by surgical indication rather than evolving renal dysfunction.
Aspirin in the later window still mainly reflects standard post-op management and does not track renal perfusion or injury. Minimal incremental predictive value for creatinine ratio.

Atorvastatin use is a chronic cardiovascular risk-management marker and not a direct AKI pathway indicator. Early post-op dosing is largely protocol/med-reconciliation driven with low renal specificity.
Statin continuation in this window is mostly a care-process/comorbidity proxy rather than a near-term physiologic precursor of AKI. Limited relevance to predicting creatinine rise by Hour 60.
Later statin dosing may weakly indicate clinical stability but has no strong mechanistic link to acute creatinine changes. High proxy penalty and low renal specificity.
Bisacodyl indicates bowel regimen/constipation management and is unrelated to renal perfusion or injury. Essentially no expected predictive value for creatinine ratio.
Bisacodyl use is a nonspecific ICU maintenance variable with no renal specificity. Any association would be incidental and confounded by length of stay/care patterns.
Bisacodyl remains a routine supportive medication and does not encode AKI risk or evolving renal dysfunction. Temporal proximity does not overcome the lack of mechanistic relevance.

Bivalirudin use early post-op may reflect HIT concern/ECMO or complex anticoagulation needs, which can correlate with severe illness and AKI risk. However it is still an indirect proxy with substantial clinician-behavior confounding.

Ongoing bivalirudin suggests complicated postoperative course (e.g., HIT/ECMO/thrombosis risk) that can be associated with renal injury, but not directly causal. Moderate proxy penalty and limited renal specificity keep the score low.

Bivalirudin in the 25–36h window can be a stronger marker of persistent critical illness/advanced support, which is more temporally proximate to subsequent creatinine rise. Still indirect (treatment-selection proxy) rather than a renal biomarker.

Bumetanide dosing reflects diuretic responsiveness/volume management and can signal congestion, low effective circulating volume, or early oliguria—relevant AKI pathways. In the immediate post-op window it is somewhat confounded by routine diuresis/resuscitation, limiting it to a moderate score.

Loop diuretic exposure often reflects volume overload/oliguria management and can signal evolving cardiorenal syndrome or early AKI. Moderately predictive but partly a clinician-response proxy and less temporally proximal than 25–36h.

Higher bumetanide requirement close to the outcome window can indicate persistent congestion, diuretic resistance, or worsening renal perfusion/AKI trajectory. Still partly treatment-driven, but timing makes it a stronger leading indicator for Hour-60 creatinine rise.

Calcium acetate is a phosphate binder; early postoperative use mainly reflects pre-existing CKD/ESRD medication continuation rather than acute kidney injury physiology. Binary administration in the immediate post-op period is a weak, behavior-driven proxy.

Ongoing phosphate binder use primarily signals chronic kidney disease management and nutrition/med reconciliation rather than acute renal function change. Limited temporal/causal proximity to Hour-60 creatinine ratio.

Later phosphate binder administration may correlate with known advanced CKD/ESRD and thus reduced renal reserve, which can influence creatinine trajectory. Still largely a chronic-condition/medication-continuation proxy rather than an AKI mechanism.

Calcium carbonate is commonly used as a supplement/antacid or binder and is not a direct marker of renal injury or perfusion. Early post-op dosing is nonspecific and heavily influenced by routine orders.

Continued calcium carbonate use is generally unrelated to acute kidney injury pathways and more reflective of baseline supplementation or GI prophylaxis practices. Weak prognostic proximity to creatinine at 60h.

Later calcium carbonate dosing remains nonspecific and does not directly encode renal function, renal perfusion, or nephrotoxic exposure. High proxy penalty with minimal expected incremental predictive value.

IV calcium chloride is often given for post-bypass hypocalcemia, transfusion-related citrate load, or hemodynamic support; it reflects acute resuscitation intensity more than kidney-specific injury. Immediate post-op derangements are common and transient, limiting specificity for Hour-60 creatinine.

Ongoing need for calcium chloride may indicate persistent instability, massive transfusion effects, or metabolic derangements, which can correlate with AKI risk indirectly. Still not kidney-specific and largely a treatment proxy.

Calcium chloride use in the 25–36h window can mark continued hemodynamic/metabolic instability closer to the creatinine outcome, which is more prognostically relevant for AKI. Association remains indirect with substantial clinician-behavior confounding.

IV calcium gluconate is used for hypocalcemia or hyperkalemia management and reflects acute metabolic/resuscitation needs rather than renal injury itself. Early postoperative administration is common and nonspecific for later creatinine rise.

Continued calcium gluconate may signal ongoing electrolyte disturbances (sometimes from renal dysfunction) but is still an indirect marker with high treatment-proxy content. Moderate temporal proximity but limited renal specificity.

In the leading-indicator window, calcium gluconate use can reflect clinically significant hyperkalemia/hypocalcemia management that may accompany evolving AKI. Predictive value is still indirect and confounded by clinician response, so not top-tier.

Carvedilol is a chronic beta-blocker; early post-op dosing mainly reflects home-med continuation and blood pressure/heart rate tolerance. It has minimal direct linkage to renal perfusion injury and high practice-pattern confounding.

Beta-blocker administration in this window is largely a marker of cardiovascular stability and medication reconciliation rather than a renal mechanism. Any association with creatinine change is weak and indirect.

Carvedilol use reflects beta-blockade for HF/HTN/AF control and overall cardiac status. It has only indirect linkage to AKI (hemodynamics/low output) and is largely a treatment-context proxy rather than a renal mechanism, though timing is relatively close.

Cefepime indicates broad-spectrum antibiotic treatment early post-op, usually reflecting suspected infection or prophylaxis. Renal relevance is indirect (illness severity; renal dosing adjustments) and the 0–12h window is prone to transient post-op confounding.

Continued cefepime suggests ongoing concern for infection or high acuity, which can correlate with AKI risk via sepsis/shock. Still mostly a clinician-behavior/indication proxy and cefepime is not a classic nephrotoxin; moderate temporal proximity.

Cefepime in the 25–36h window may better capture evolving infection/sepsis physiology closer to the creatinine outcome and may also reflect renal dose reduction as kidney function worsens. Predictive value is still indirect but timing makes it a stronger signal than earlier epochs.

Ceftriaxone use is typically prophylaxis or empiric coverage and is not strongly nephrotoxic. In the immediate post-op window it mainly reflects routine practice patterns rather than renal injury risk.
Ongoing ceftriaxone suggests continued antibiotic coverage but remains weakly tied to AKI mechanisms. It is largely an indication/behavior proxy with minimal direct renal specificity.

Ceftriaxone closer to the outcome may reflect evolving infection or complications that can secondarily affect renal function. However it remains nonspecific and not a strong renal-pathway marker, so importance stays low.

Clonidine use reflects sedation/analgesia adjunct or BP control and can contribute to hypotension/bradycardia. Renal impact is indirect via perfusion and the early post-op period is confounded by anesthesia and resuscitation.
